title: Intestinal worms and how to deal with them
excerpt: Intestinal worms, called Krimi in Ayurveda, are more common than most people admit. The classical texts offer a clear picture of why they take hold and what gentle daily habits help keep them away.
at_a_glance: Krimi (intestinal worms) thrive when Agni is weak and undigested food accumulates in the gut
Reducing sweet, heavy, and fermented foods removes the environment worms depend on
Basic daily hygiene, trimmed nails, and clean water are the most reliable preventive habits
agni_desc: The goal is a steady, balanced fire (sama). Too low leaves food undigested; too sharp burns through it. An irregular fire (vishama) swings between the two.

body.heading: an uncomfortable truth most families know
paragraphs: At some point, almost every household in India has dealt with the tell-tale signs: a child who scratches restlessly at night, an adult with a persistently unsettled stomach, or an appetite that swings between ravenous and absent. These are often the quiet signals of intestinal worms, a problem people tend to dismiss or treat with a single dose of medicine and then forget about. Ayurveda looked at this condition more thoroughly, and what the classical texts say about it is still practically useful today.

The Sanskrit term is Krimi, meaning organisms that inhabit and disturb the body from within. The word covers a broad category that includes parasitic worms of the digestive tract as well as other microscopic organisms. Caraka Samhita devotes an entire chapter to Krimi, classifying them by the part of the body they affect and the conditions that allow them to multiply (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 7). This is not a fringe topic in the classical literature. It is treated as a serious clinical problem with identifiable causes.
body.pullQuote: the gut's ecology matters as much as the external source of infection

body.heading: what creates the conditions for worms
paragraphs: Ayurveda does not see worms as purely an external invasion. They arise, or at least flourish, because the internal environment has become hospitable to them. The primary cause is weak or disturbed Agni, the digestive fire. When food is not properly broken down, undigested material accumulates in the gut and becomes, in the language of the texts, Ama, a sticky, fermenting residue. This residue is exactly what intestinal parasites feed on and breed in.

Caraka Samhita lists the dietary habits that contribute to this environment: excessive consumption of sweet and heavy foods, eating before the previous meal is fully digested, sleeping immediately after meals, and eating food that is sour, fermented, or improperly stored (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 7). Kashyapa Samhita, a text especially focused on children's health, gives particular attention to Krimi in infants and young children and notes that improper feeding practices and poor hygiene in the child's environment are significant contributing factors (Kashyapa Samhita, Kalpasthana).

The Bhavaprakasha, a later but widely respected text, echoes this and adds that a sedentary lifestyle and suppression of natural urges such as timely bowel clearance also disturb the gut environment enough to allow parasites to take hold (Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda). The common thread across all three texts is that the gut's ecology matters as much as the external source of infection.

body.heading: traditional foods and practices that help
paragraphs: The classical remedy most often mentioned for Krimi is Vidanga, the seeds of Embelia ribes. Bhavaprakasha's Nighantu describes Vidanga as having a specifically anthelmintic quality, meaning it discourages worm activity in the gut without being harsh on the system itself. It is one of the ingredients in traditional formulations still available at Ayurvedic pharmacies today. It is worth knowing about for awareness, though any use should ideally be guided by a qualified vaidya.

Beyond specific herbs, the classical texts emphasize diet as the primary tool. Reducing sweet, heavy, and fermented foods removes the substrate worms thrive on. Bitter vegetables and bitter gourd in particular are mentioned across several texts as unfavorable to Krimi. Raw garlic, used in cooking or as a small raw dose on an empty stomach, has a long history in classical and folk traditions as a mild anthelmintic. Caraka Samhita notes that foods with a naturally pungent or bitter taste tend to be inhospitable to these organisms (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 26).

Hygiene, though expressed in the language of the time, is a consistent concern in these texts. Washing hands before meals, keeping food covered, avoiding water from stagnant sources, and bathing regularly are all recommended as Dinacharya, daily routine practices, in Ashtanga Hridaya's chapter on daily regimen (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2). These align completely with what modern medicine recommends for preventing parasitic infections.

For children especially, keeping nails trimmed short is a simple but effective habit. Parasites often re-enter the body through hand-to-mouth contact after scratching. Kashyapa Samhita's attention to children's environments reflects an understanding that prevention in daily habits matters as much as treatment after the fact.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: This article is general information for awareness, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. If you or your child has persistent stomach pain, visible worms in the stool, significant weight loss, anaemia, or extreme fatigue, please see a qualified doctor or paediatrician promptly. Children who are not gaining weight normally, or who show signs of a distended abdomen, also need medical evaluation. A vaidya experienced in classical Ayurveda can offer additional guidance on herbal support, but a confirmed parasitic infection, especially in young children, benefits from accurate diagnosis first. Over-the-counter anthelmintic medication is widely available and effective, and there is no reason to delay seeking it when symptoms are clear.

try_this_today.title: Bitter gourd once a week
try_this_today.description: Include bitter gourd (karela) in a weekly meal. Its bitter taste is specifically noted in classical texts as inhospitable to intestinal parasites.

try_this_today.title: Small raw garlic in the morning
try_this_today.description: One small clove of raw garlic crushed and swallowed with warm water on an empty stomach is a traditional and widely used anthelmintic practice. Avoid this if you have acidity or gastritis.

try_this_today.title: Trim children's nails regularly
try_this_today.description: Short nails reduce the chance of parasite eggs surviving under the fingernails and re-entering through hand-to-mouth contact, which is the main route of reinfection.

try_this_today.title: Do not eat before the previous meal has digested
try_this_today.description: Caraka Samhita lists eating on top of undigested food as a direct cause of the gut conditions that allow Krimi to multiply. A simple rule: if you are not genuinely hungry, wait.

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.description: Supports digestive fire and helps clear Ama that creates a hospitable environment for Krimi

words_to_know.definition: A broad classical category covering intestinal worms and other organisms that disturb the body from within
The digestive fire, responsible for breaking down food and maintaining a healthy gut environment
Undigested, toxic residue that accumulates when Agni is weak, creating conditions favorable to Krimi
Seeds of Embelia ribes, a classical anthelmintic herb described in Bhavaprakasha and still used in Ayurvedic formulations
The Ayurvedic daily routine, including hygiene practices that prevent disease

sources: Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 7 (Krimichikitsa) · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu (Vidanga); Bhavaprakasha (general) · Kashyapa Samhita, (Krimi in children) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2 (Dinacharya)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
